title: Cost Planning
description: Quiz covering cost estimating, budgeting, cost control, estimating techniques, reserves, cost baselines and earned value management.

questions.question: What is the primary objective of project cost planning?
options: To estimate, budget and control the costs required to complete the project
questions.explanation: Cost planning ensures that sufficient financial resources are available and managed throughout the project lifecycle.

questions.question: Which activity comes first in the project cost management process?
questions.explanation: Before creating a budget, the project team must first estimate the costs of the individual activities.

questions.question: What is a project cost estimate?
options: The final amount the project will definitely cost
An informed prediction of the expected cost to perform project work
A record of actual project expenses
questions.explanation: A cost estimate predicts the expected cost based on available information before the project begins.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a project budget?
options: To combine approved cost estimates into an authorized spending plan
questions.explanation: The project budget consolidates estimated costs into an approved financial plan for executing the project.

questions.question: Which statement best describes cost control?
options: Estimating the project's total cost before work begins
Monitoring actual costs and managing deviations from the approved budget
questions.explanation: Cost control involves tracking project spending and taking corrective actions when costs differ from the plan.

questions.question: A project is very similar to another completed by the company last year. Which estimating technique would typically be the most appropriate to use first?
questions.explanation: Analogous estimating uses historical information from similar completed projects to produce a quick estimate.

questions.question: Why is Bottom-Up Estimating generally more accurate than Analogous Estimating?
options: It estimates each work package individually before combining them
It always produces the lowest project cost
It only requires one previous project for comparison
questions.explanation: Bottom-Up Estimating evaluates each activity separately, resulting in greater detail and usually higher accuracy.

questions.question: Which estimating technique uses measurable relationships, such as cost per square meter or cost per kilometer, to calculate project costs?
questions.explanation: Parametric estimating applies statistical relationships between historical data and project variables.

questions.question: Three-Point Estimating improves cost estimates because it:
options: Uses only the lowest possible estimate
Considers optimistic, most likely and pessimistic scenarios
Requires the project to be completed first
questions.explanation: Three-Point Estimating accounts for uncertainty by evaluating multiple possible outcomes rather than relying on a single estimate.

questions.question: Which of the following would normally be classified as a direct project cost?
options: Concrete purchased specifically for a construction project
questions.explanation: Direct costs can be directly assigned to the project, such as project-specific materials, labor and equipment.

questions.question: Which of the following is typically considered an indirect project cost?
options: Office rent for the company's headquarters
Concrete used for the project foundation
questions.explanation: Indirect costs support multiple projects or the organization as a whole and cannot be directly assigned to a single project.

questions.question: A software company pays a fixed monthly office lease regardless of how many projects are running. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Fixed costs remain constant regardless of project activity or production levels.

questions.question: Fuel costs for construction equipment increase as the equipment is used more frequently. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Variable costs change depending on the level of project activity.

questions.question: A project team identifies the possibility that material prices could increase by 8% during construction. Which reserve should normally be included in the project budget?
questions.explanation: Contingency reserves are established to cover known risks that have been identified during project planning.

questions.question: During execution, a completely unexpected government regulation requires major redesign work that was not identified as a project risk. Which reserve would most likely be used?
questions.explanation: Management reserves cover unforeseen events and unknown risks that were not included in the original risk register.

questions.question: Why is it important to distinguish between contingency reserves and management reserves?
options: Because they are approved and controlled differently
Because they always have the same purpose
Because contingency reserves are never included in project planning
Because management reserves replace the project budget
questions.explanation: Contingency reserves are planned for identified risks, while management reserves are typically controlled separately for unknown events.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Cost Baseline?
options: To provide the approved budget against which project performance is measured
questions.explanation: The Cost Baseline serves as the approved reference for comparing planned and actual project costs.

questions.question: A project manager notices that actual spending is higher than planned during the first three months of the project. What should the project manager do first?
options: Compare actual costs with the Cost Baseline and determine the cause of the variance
Reduce the project scope without consulting stakeholders
questions.explanation: Before taking corrective action, the project manager should understand why the variance occurred and whether it is likely to continue.

questions.question: Which statement best describes cost monitoring during project execution?
options: Costs are only reviewed when the project finishes
Actual spending is continuously compared with planned spending to identify deviations early
Cost monitoring is only necessary for construction projects
Budgets should never be updated after approval
questions.explanation: Regular cost monitoring helps detect problems early and allows corrective actions before significant budget overruns occur.

questions.question: Which project management technique integrates scope, schedule and cost performance into one measurement system?
questions.explanation: Earned Value Management combines scope, schedule and cost information to evaluate overall project performance and forecast future outcomes.

questions.question: In Earned Value Management (EVM), what does Planned Value (PV) represent?
options: The actual amount of money spent on the project
The estimated value of the work that should have been completed by a specific date
The value of completed work based on actual progress
questions.explanation: Planned Value (PV) is the authorized budget assigned to the work that was scheduled to be completed by a given point in time.

questions.question: What does Actual Cost (AC) measure?
options: The budgeted cost of completed work
The money that has actually been spent to perform the work completed
questions.explanation: Actual Cost (AC) is the real amount spent on the project up to the reporting date.

questions.question: What does Earned Value (EV) represent?
options: The amount of money remaining in the budget
The value of the work actually completed, expressed in terms of the approved budget
The amount spent on project resources
questions.explanation: Earned Value measures how much planned work has actually been completed, using the approved budget as the reference.

questions.question: A project has an Earned Value (EV) of $90,000 and an Actual Cost (AC) of $100,000. What does this indicate?
options: The project is ahead of schedule
The project has no cost variance
questions.explanation: Since EV is lower than AC, the project has spent more than the value of the work completed and is therefore over budget.

questions.question: A project has an Earned Value (EV) of $120,000 and an Actual Cost (AC) of $100,000. Which statement is correct?
options: The project has exhausted its contingency reserve
questions.explanation: When EV exceeds AC, the project has delivered more value than it has spent, indicating it is under budget.

questions.question: A project has a Planned Value (PV) of $150,000 but an Earned Value (EV) of only $120,000. What does this suggest?
options: The project is ahead of schedule
The project has exceeded its budget
questions.explanation: If EV is lower than PV, less work has been completed than was planned, indicating the project is behind schedule.

questions.question: Why do many organizations use Earned Value Management instead of simply comparing actual spending with the budget?
options: Because EVM evaluates both cost and project progress at the same time
Because EVM eliminates all project risks
Because EVM guarantees projects finish on time
Because EVM replaces the project schedule
questions.explanation: Simply spending less money does not necessarily mean a project is performing well. EVM considers both spending and actual work completed.

questions.question: A project manager reports that the project has spent only 60% of its budget, but only 40% of the planned work has been completed. What is the best interpretation?
options: The project is performing well because less money has been spent
The project may actually be behind schedule despite lower spending
The project has exceeded its budget
The project should immediately be closed
questions.explanation: Low spending alone is not necessarily positive if progress is even lower than expected.

questions.question: Which statement best summarizes good project cost management?
options: Create a budget once and avoid changing it
Focus only on minimizing project costs
Estimate costs realistically, monitor performance continuously, and respond to deviations early
Spend the entire contingency reserve before project completion
questions.explanation: Successful cost management combines realistic planning with continuous monitoring and timely corrective action.

questions.question: A project sponsor asks whether the project is healthy. Which answer provides the most complete assessment?
options: We have spent less money than expected.
We should evaluate cost, schedule and completed work together using measures such as Earned Value Management.
questions.explanation: A project's health cannot be judged from cost or schedule alone. Earned Value Management combines cost, schedule and scope performance to provide a complete picture of project performance.
